The system uses time "buckets" to hold the counts. It adds new events to the current bucket. The system only checks the time when a new event arrives. It does not check constantly. This design saves computer power. When enough time passes, the system drops the oldest bucket. This keeps the total count fresh and up-to-date. The tool can track a single total count. It can also track separate counts for many different keys. Overview and Purpose Internal Role This mechanism provides the foundational logic for tracking event frequency over a moving time interval. It is a core component within fbcode/admarket/lib/sampling, enabling rate measurement (e.g., "events per hour") on a rolling basis. This functionality is fundamental for downstream systems that perform rate limiting and volume control. Sources: admarket/lib/sampling/AtomicSlidingWindowCounter.h, admarket/lib/sampling/KeyedSlidingWindowCounter.h Core Logic The system divides a total time duration into a series of discrete "buckets." Events are counted within the current bucket. As time progresses, a "slider" mechanism advances the current bucket index, and a callback is triggered to discard the oldest bucket's count from the total. This creates a continuously updated count of recent activity. Sources: admarket/lib/sampling/SlidingWindowSlider.cpp:15-19, admarket/lib/sampling/AtomicSlidingWindowCounter.cpp:27-34 Key Concepts Breakdown Time-Based Bucketing The SlidingWindowSlider class manages the progression of time through a circular series of bucket indices. It is responsible for calculating when to advance to the next bucket and executing a callback to notify a listener of the shift. This provides the "sliding" aspect of the window. Sources: admarket/lib/sampling/SlidingWindowSlider.h:26-33 Lazy Bucket Advancement Instead of using a background thread, the bucket index is advanced through a periodic check within getCurrentBucketIndex. This check is performed only every kBucketChangeCheckFrequency calls to optimize performance by avoiding constant time queries. This design trades perfect timing accuracy for lower system overhead. Dan Melnic: I can add a sleep too in between Dan Melnic: That didn't really work - but this did: ``` + // eventBaseWatchdog->disableRateLimiter(); + eventBaseWatchdog->setRateLimit(1000000*kNumThreads); ``` Dan Melnic: #thanks @[100013779345452:2048:Dan Shiovitz] for explaining the folly::TokenBucket behavior and providing suggestions on how to fix the tests _Created: 2024-02-13T11:02:16Z | Updated: 2024-02-13T12:20:42Z_ ## [wp learnings](https://www.internalfb.com/wiki/Users/zutianluo/fbcode/wp_learnings/) Cheapest timing function folly::chrono::coarse_steady_clock is the cheapest at the millisecond level. std::chrono::steady_clock is the best for nanosecond measurements, and folly::chrono::hardware_timestamp()is the fastest for clock cycles. Sync conf thrift changes to fbcode: configerator-thrift-updater Easily use sum types (AKA "or" types) in C++: std::variant and folly::variant_match link _Created: 2019-06-20T08:43:02Z | Updated: 2022-04-14T07:24:58Z_ ## [Learned in C++ about folly::ConcurrentHashMap](https://fb.workplace.com/groups/2310942935628531/permalink/3459602260762587/) Post Content: # Learned in C++ about `folly::ConcurrentHashMap` ## tldr: `folly::ConcurrentHashMap` is providing synchronization only over the map structure itself. But it is not thread safe for its mapped values. ## Beginning of the story As working on the event processor rate limiter. I start to learn more about c++. In the rate limiter, I need a hashmap as counters to store how many events we get for each event type. There are multiple threads will read/write the values from the hashmap. So I have to take care of the concurrency of it. By doing a quick search, I find a [wiki about Concurrency in C++](https://www.internalfb.com/intern/wiki/Concurrency/#data-structures). And it mentions about the `folly::ConcurrentHashMap`, which is easy to use and "completely thread safe". So here I build my local counter as following. ``` folly::ConcurrentHashMap localCounter_; ``` ## Seeing the issue Everything looks fine until after I committed my unit tests for the rate limiter. The tests are getting flaky, then I start to debug it. Because most of the time, the tests are good. I start to think if the update of the counter doesn't work as expected? By searching on google, find [this message on GitHub](https://github.com/facebook/folly/issues/1185#issuecomment-508242911). > ConcurrentHashMap provides no synchronization over the mapped values. It provides synchronization only over the map structure itself. For example, it permits concurrent access to a value in one thread while another thread erases that value. Yeah, it makes sense, I just misunderstand it at the beginning. Then I decided to run some quick experiments to make sure I really know the behavior. ## experiments The setup is simple, there are 10 threads that will update the same value in the `folly::ConcurrentHashMap` for 100 times. So the total count would be 1000 at the end. **Code snippets:** ```cpp folly::ConcurrentHashMap localCounter_; void runThread() { for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++) { localCounter_.assign_if_equal(0, localCounter_[0], localCounter_[0] + 1); } } int main(int argc, char** argv) { localCounter_.emplace(0, make_shared(0)); vector threads{}; for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) { threads.emplace_back(thread([]() { runThread(); })); } for (auto& t : threads) { if (t.joinable()) { t.join(); } } cout << "Total: " << localCounter_[0] << endl; } ``` **Run result:** ``` Building: finished in 3.2 sec (100%) 3358/3358 jobs, 0 updated Total time: 4.3 sec Total: 276 ``` The total count is only 276. Clearly I have a problem here. The fix here could be add a mutex for each value in the hashmap. But adding a mutex would be slow and complex. I decided to make the value atomic. **Code snippets:** ```cpp folly::ConcurrentHashMap> localCounter_; void runThread() { for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++) { localCounter_.at(0)->fetch_add(1); } } int main(int argc, char** argv) { localCounter_.emplace(0, make_shared(0)); vector threads{}; for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) { threads.emplace_back(thread([]() { runThread(); })); } for (auto& t : threads) { if (t.joinable()) { t.join(); } } cout << "Total: " << localCounter_[0]->load() << endl; } ``` **Run result:** ``` Building: finished in 17.1 sec (100%) 4679/4679 jobs, 2 updated Total time: 18.1 sec Total: 1000 ``` Yes! I got 1000. By changing the value type to `shared_ptr`, I think it's perfectly fit my use case right now. ## follow-up question Here could be one question that why I used a `shared_ptr` instead directly use `atomic_int`? It because `folly::ConcurrentHashMap` will return a copy of value for keeping the thread safe on the data structure, but the `std::atomic` is neither copyable nor moveable. So getting the copy of a shared pointer is a good solution for me. ## about this post I pretty sure many of you already know these pretty well. But I hope this can help someone like me who is picking up the c++. As I am also using `folly::Singleton` a lot in the rate limiter. I think I can share some of my experience with `folly::Singleton` later. Comments (format: : ): Yue Cai: Thanks for sharing this! Really an easy to understand read for someone like me who is a complete n00b to C++. one question, from the updated test results of using sharedPtr, the actual test run time is (18.1 - 17.1)= 1s right? Zach Li: That's a good question. Actually I missed one line of output XD. That 1 second is for parsing buck files. There is no information about run time. ``` [zehuali@38940.od ~/fbsource/fbcode (1028bd5e)]$ buck run @mode/dev //admarket/adtable/event/ads_prod_infra/ratelimiter:HelloWorld Parsing buck files: finished in 1.0 sec Building: finished in 17.1 sec (100%) 4679/4679 jobs, 2 updated Total time: 18.1 sec Hello, World! Total: 1000 ``` Yue Cai: @[100040023083381:2048:Zach]: I am not familiar with buck :P I was just wondering if there is any performance issue if we update to use atomic int? since looks like the test total time changed from ~3s to ~18s and i am not sure if the 'building' time is test run time? Zach Li: @[100010204507572:2048:Yue]: I think there should be some performance difference because when using the atomic or not, as we force each thread to make the update one by one. It will require to set up another benchmark to test it 😝. I find [one benchmark from online](http://demin.ws/blog/english/2012/05/05/atomic-spinlock-mutex/), I think the atomic is relative less expensive. And yeah, that ~3s and ~18s are just the time to compile the cpp code. Xuli Liu: `folly::Synchronized` is my favorite Zach Li: Yeah, I'm still not sure how to choose the right lock for different use cases. Will take a deeper study. _Created: 2020-09-14T16:53:28Z | Updated: 2020-09-15T12:37:03Z_ ## [How difficult would it be to integrate coroutine debug info with folly executors?](https://fb.workplace.com/groups/192968587972839/permalink/921795461756811/) Post Content: # How difficult would it be to integrate coroutine debug info with folly executors? Currently, the `co_bt` gdb extension will only show coroutines if they are actively running on threads. However, there are use cases where we may want to see suspended coroutines as well. **Use case: see all suspended coroutines on an executor/across all executors. Example:** - Suppose I have a live process or a core dump - I want to debug why some async work is not completing - The coroutines performing the async work is currently suspended, and I don't know why the coroutines are not resuming - I would like to see all the coroutines that are currently suspended, and then examine the async stack traces of those suspended coroutines - Workflow might look something like this ``` # Command that would be nice to have: # list all pointers to executors (gdb) co_exec_ls 0xABCDABCD 0xDEADDEAD ... # Command that would be nice to have: # Given an executor, list all pointers to coroutines # attached to that executor (gdb) co_exec_co_ls 0xDEADDEAD 0xCAFECAFE 0xFECAFECA ... # Command that would be nice to have: # Show back traces of all coroutines attached # to an executor (gdb) co_exec_co_bt_all 0xDEADDEAD ... all async stack traces ... # Command that already exists: # Show the async back trace given # a pointer to a specific coroutine/async stack frame (gdb) co_bt 0xCAFECAFE ... async stack trace ... # Command that would be nice to have: # Given a pointer to a coroutine, # get the associated executor (gdb) co_exec_get 0xCAFECAFE 0xDEADDEAD ``` How difficult would it be to add support for this? I'm thinking: - We already track `AsyncStackFrame` and `Executor::KeepAlive` [within `folly::coro::TaskPromiseBase`](https://www.internalfb.com/code/fbsource/[ec424a76752f4bcd93e0649796fc8ccd0d2e59ac]/fbcode/folly/experimental/coro/Task.h?lines=135-137). - This means that given a pointer to an `AsyncStackFrame`, we could probably do some pointer arithmetic to get back the pointer to `TaskPromiseBase` and therefore the `Executor::KeepAlive` and `Executor` - For [`CPUThreadPoolExecutor`](https://www.internalfb.com/code/fbsource/[df3a4b6bc14ecee08b6ec5c9cafd526b1bf8aeab]/fbcode/folly/executors/CPUThreadPoolExecutor.h?lines=200), we could poke at the internals of `BlockingQueue` (backed by `MPMCQueue`) and try to examine the entries within the queue there, but that might require more additional metadata to be tracked to understand the internals of the lambda provided to `CPUTask` to know that its a coroutine - For [`IOThreadPoolExecutor`](https://www.internalfb.com/code/fbsource/[df3a4b6bc14ecee08b6ec5c9cafd526b1bf8aeab]/fbcode/folly/executors/IOThreadPoolExecutor.h?lines=95), we could poke at the internals of `folly::EventBase`, but we probably also need additional metadata to know the coroutine context of the work provided to the event base - For other implementations of `folly::Executor`, I'm not sure if there's any generic way to list all coroutines associated with the executor Is this use case a good idea? If so, what might be a way to track the information necessary to provide these debugging tools? Comments (format: : ): Kenny Yu: cc @[100027634658781:2048:Shankar], @[100026145637303:2048:Harrison], @[100010463798539:2048:Lee] Cameron Pickett: Somewhat related: I would personally love a tool like top, but for coroutine tasks. Imagine if every task looked like a process does in ‘top’ and you could scroll through the list and see which are blocked, which are running, etc. I could see any of these as being incredibly useful to debug coroutine deadlock Kenny Yu: Bumping this thread, as I think having this debug info would be very valuable. However, I'm not sure what the right way of doing this is to avoid perf implications. Some questions/thoughts: - (1) Ideally, we have an easy way of getting "all coroutines that exist, and their current state running/suspended/runnable". - Ideally this would return a list of tuples representing each coroutine, where each tuple contains: - pointer to top async stack frame which allows us to walk the async stack - current state of the coroutine: `RUNNING | RUNNABLE | SUSPENDED | TERMINATED` - pointer to executor that holds the coroutine - if the coroutine is running, the current `rip` and `rbp` registers to get the normal stack frames as well (in case the async stack frame has called normal functions) - In addition, the ideal approach should require none/minimal changes by developers to enable this functionality, and should not add significant performance regressions - If we had this information, then we could get the following debugging tools: - gdb command to print all coroutines, even the suspended ones, and examine why they are suspended - implement a `co_top`-like CLI tool to show coroutine state live, cc @[100008629577644:2048:Cameron]. I think this could probably be implemented efficiently using a FOLLY_SDT trace point + bpf uprobe program - helper functions to print suspended coroutines on demand - (2) I'm not sure if there's an easy way of doing (1) that doesn't involve too much overhead though. A naive implementation that may add overhead is: - have a global list of pointers - the global list would require some synchronization - every time a new folly::coro::Task/Generator/AsyncGenerator is created or destroyed, update the global list - (3) perhaps a slightly less expensive approach might be: - have a global list of executors - every time a new executor is created/destroyed (not frequent), add it to the global list - executors keep a list of queued coroutine tasks - folly:coro::Task/Generator/AsyncGenerator is enqueued/dequeued from an executor, the executor updates its list (should be not much synchronization overhead beyond what Executor::add already does) - (4) other options? cc @[100022391923209:2048:Yedidya], @[100010722100724:2048:Andrii], @[100010463798539:2048:Lee] do you have ideas on how we might be able to get this information in a performant way? Yedidya Feldblum: @[100019362581475:2048:Kenny Yu] @[100022787660955:2048:Nicholas Ormrod] Lee Howes: We may not want all coroutines. That would end up listing every one in a stack. We don't just want queued coroutines, either, because that will not tell us which ones are waiting. We'd probably need to add to a data structure at every `co_await` of a truly async operation that isn't itself a coroutine, and the enqueue of the coroutine's resume callback in an executor queue. It does sound expensive. Maybe a global intrusive doubly linked list that's a minor extension of the current chaining? Yedidya Feldblum: One thing I would be worried about is the runtime performance hit from global lists and the synchronizations required for them. And a bit the binary size increase of all the tracking. ...46913 bytes truncated... The tool call succeeded but the output was truncated. Based on my searches, I found that **BucketizedCounter does NOT appear to exist as a class in folly**. The search results show:

1. **No class definition found** in folly's codebase for `BucketizedCounter`
2. References found are in **www code** (PHP), not C++ folly:
   - `www/flib/profiling/latprof/v2/global_metrics/BucketizedCounterLatProfGlobalMetricSampler.php`
3. What IS in folly are related classes like:
   - `folly::BucketedTimeSeries` (used in sampling/limiting code)
   - `folly::AtomicHashMap` 
   - Classes in `admarket/lib/sampling/` like `AtomicSlidingWindowCounter` and `KeyedSlidingWindowCounter` (which use bucketing internally)

The term "BucketizedCounter" appears to be:
- A **PHP class** in www's LatProf system, not a C++ folly class
- A **generic concept** that various systems implement differently
- Not an official folly data structure

If you're looking for bucketed counting in folly, you might want:
- **`folly::BucketedTimeSeries`** - for time-series bucketed data
- **`AtomicSlidingWindowCounter`** or **`KeyedSlidingWindowCounter`** in `fbcode/admarket/lib/sampling/` - for sliding window counts
